February 26, 1982 
Peter Douglas 
Chief Deputy Director 
California Coastal Commission 
631 Howard Street, 4th Floor 
San Francisco, CA 94105 
RE: 
Lynn Heacox 
FPPC ~o. GC-81/l265 
Dear Mr. Douglas: 
Enforcement 
322·0«1 
5tatetnents of Economic lnterei1 
3226444 
We have completed our evaluation of the potential Polit-
ical Reform Act {the Act) violations you referred to in your 
December 15, 1981, letter to Lynn Heacox. 
In that letter, 
vou exoressed concern that Mr. Heacox, a former permit ana-
lyst f;r the California Coastal Commission, may h3ve violated 
Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Act (Government Code 
Sections 87..+01, 87402) V. Those provisions generally pro-
hibit a former state employee from representing a private 
party before a state agency regarding a matter on which the 
former employee worked while employed by the state. 
In order 
for there to have been a violation of those sections, 
however, the former employee must be compensated for his rep-
resentation by the private party. 
Our evaluation of ~r. 
Heacox's representation activities in question has determined 
that he received no compensation from the private parties 
involved. 
~herefore his actions thus far in regard to those 
parties' building permit applications before the California 
Coastal Crnnmission do not constitute violations of the Act. 
Nevertheless, Mr. Heacox has indicated to us that he wishes 
to do compensated work for one of the private parties by 
presenting a new permit application to the Commission on be-
half of that party. 
For his benefit and yours we therefore 
offer the following advice as to when a former permit analyst 
should not accept employment with private parties who have 
matters pending before the California Coascal Commission. 
_1/ 
-
All statu~ory references are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise noted. 


Peter Douglas 
February 26, 1982 
Page 2 
Taken together, Sections 87401 and 87402 prevent a for-
mer state employee, after leaving state employment, from 
acting as the representative for or assisting in the repre-
sentation of a person in a state agency proceeding in which 
the former state employee "participated" while a state 
employee. 
"Participated" is defined in Section 87400(d) as 
meaning "to have taken part personally and substantially 
through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, 
investigation or use of confidential information as an offi-
cer or employee . 
"The g~neral purposes of the 
prohibitions of Secticns 87401 and 87402 are contained in a 
February 21, 1979, memorandum to ':h€: Fair Politic)l Practices 
Commissioners from the Commission staff. 
T~e memorandum con-
tained a proposed draft of Sections 87400-87405 stating that 
these new statutes were attempting to embody certain broad 
principles of policy: 
1. 
The interests of the government .3nd the 
public as a former client should be protected 
by preventing the disclosure or use of matters 
revealed by reason of a relationship having 
confidential aspects. 
2. 
'rhe integrity of the goverflJtlental 
decision-making process should be protected by 
reducing the chance that post-government em-
ployment considerations will bias the conduct 
of goverrment business. 
3. 
Both the interests of the government and 
the public and the integrity of the 
governmental decision-making process should be 
protected by limitations on the opportunity 
for undue advantage by reason of familiarity 
with age~cy practice and procedure and person-
al relationships with agency personnel. 
Against these principles must be balanced: 
1. 
The ability of the goverr~ent to recruit 
competent personnel whose career objectives 
will require an assessment of the 
disadvantages of government se::::vice. 
2. 
The acvar:tage to efficient government 
operations of having participants in 
government3l proceedings who are acquainted 
with practice, procedure, and precedent. 


Peter Dougl21s 
February 26, 1982 
Page :3 
3. 
The right of private parties to obtain 
competent representatives of their ow~ choice. 
As a general rule, therefore, a former California 
Coastal Commission permit analyst may not accept employment 
with a private party in regard to a development site permit 
application, or other related proceeding, pending before the 
California Coastal Commission when the former permit analyst 
has "personally and substantially participated" in the prior 
permit history for development on that particular site. 
As 
noted above, the term "personally and substantially" is used 
in Section 87400(d) to describe a state employee's participa-
tion in matters reviewed by his state agency emplo~er. 
The 
types of activities that usually will constitute personal and 
substantial involvement by a permit analyst are described in 
the State Personnel Board's specifications sheet for "Coastal 
Program Analyst," (permit analyst): 
Work assignments involve planning, 
regul~t~n9' 
and controlling development of the coast; aavlslng 
permit applicants on the planning, technical and 
legal aspects of their proposed projects, consult-
ing with local, regional, State, Federal planning 
officials and with the public to coordinate and 
intagrate regulatory and planning activities~ 
determining the conformity of proposed coastal de-
velopment with standards of the California Coastal 
Act of 1976; assisting local goverrunents in the 
preparation of local coastal progcams required by 
the Coastal Act~ reviewing local coastal programs 
to determine whether they have been developed and 
are being carried out in accordance with State and 
Feder~l laws, and preoaring recommendations to the 
State Coastal Commission and six regional Coastal 
Commissions to guide the Commissions to their for-
mal actions on development permits and appeals, 
coastal planning, and local coastal programs. 
This list is not necessarily exhaustive of all the per-
mit history activities in which a permit analyst may person-
ally and substantially engage that would limit his future 
private employment opportunities. 
~loreover there mav be 
exceptions wher~ participation in one of the above wbrk 
assignments for' a particular project would not prevent future 
private employment on the same project. 
See Section 87403. 


Pe:er DCt:;q las 
FEbruary ~61 1982 
Fdge 4 
Thus since the prohibitions of Sections 87401 and 8740~ 
require a subjective test to be applied in determining possi-
ble violations, each situation must be reviewed on an indi-
vidual basis. 
Former California Coastal Commission employees 
therefore should contact the California Coastal Commission 
legal staff for guidance as to what specific permit analyst 
activities constitute perso~al and substantial participation 
for purposes of Sections 87401 and 87402. 
The 
Coastal Commission then may look to the FPPC's Legal Division 
for assistance in interpreting the intent and definitions of 
the terms of these sections. 
Weighing the facts of each case 
in light of the broad policy considerations set forth above 
should lead to a correct and fair determination. 
If I can be of any further help in this particular 
matter, please do not hesitate to contact me. 
SRC:s[ 
cc:Mr. Lynn Heacox 
Si ncere ly, 
j4U/l ,/ /' ~,

Steven R. Crooks 
Legal Counsel 
Enforcement Division 
7071 Betty Drive 
Huntington Beach, CA 92647

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a former California Coastal Commission permit analyst violated Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Political Reform Act by representing private parties in permit matters before the Commission, and when such representation would be prohibited if compensation were involved.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The former permit analyst's uncompensated representation activities did not violate the Act because compensation is required for a violation. However, the FPPC provides guidance that a former permit analyst generally cannot accept compensated employment with a private party regarding a development site permit application pending before the Commission when the analyst personally and substantially participated in the prior permit history for that site.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a cleanly structured FPPC advice letter with two pages. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but content clearly follows advice letter structure with factual background, legal analysis, and advisory guidance. Minor OCR degradation in some sections but core content legible. Document references Government Code Sections 87401-87405 and Political Reform Act provisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a former California Coastal Commission permit analyst violated Sections 87401 and/or 87402 of the Political Reform Act by representing private parties in permit matters before the Commission, and when such representation would be prohibited if compensation were involved.

CONCLUSION: The former permit analyst's uncompensated representation activities did not violate the Act because compensation is required for a violation. However, the FPPC provides guidance that a former permit analyst generally cannot accept compensated employment with a private party regarding a development site permit application pending before the Commission when the analyst personally and substantially participated in the prior permit history for that site.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to an inquiry about whether former California Coastal Commission permit analyst Lynn Heacox violated the Political Reform Act by representing private parties in permit matters. The FPPC determined no violation occurred for uncompensated representation but provided guidance that compensated representation would be prohibited when the former analyst previously participated substantially in matters concerning that same project.
